questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The back cover helped her know what was inside. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Milo wants to see the name of his book. But the front cover fell off and is gone! So Milo opens the book to the very first page inside. The name of the book is printed there too.

Which PART of the book is Milo reading now?
questions.hint: The front cover is gone, so cross it out. Where else does the book's name show up? Think about what the FIRST page inside is called.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Milo is reading the title page, the first page inside. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, the front cover is gone. Milo opened to the first page inside. What is that page?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Milo is reading the title page. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Pia wants to know the book's name AND see its big picture, and she wants to do it WITHOUT opening the book.

Which ONE part shows her all of that?
questions.hint: Two parts show the name, but one of them makes you open the book first. Pick the part you can see while the book is still closed.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The front cover shows the name AND a big picture, without opening the book. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Pia did NOT open the book. Which outside part has both the name and a picture?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The front cover shows both the name and the picture. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Ben is holding a book. He already looked at the front cover. Then he turns just ONE page. Now he sees the name of the book and the name of the person who wrote it.

What is this page called?
questions.hint: He has ALREADY passed the front cover, so it can't be that. What is the very first page inside a book called?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Ben turned to the title page. It shows the name and who wrote it. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Ben turned just one page past the front. What is that page called?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! It is the title page. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Nan wants to find out what a book is about. She does NOT look at the front. She flips the whole book over and reads the very last OUTSIDE part.

Which PART is Nan reading?
questions.hint: She flipped the book OVER to the outside, not to a page inside. Which cover is on the opposite side from the front?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The back cover is the very last outside part of the book. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, Nan flipped the whole book over to the outside back. Which part is that?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Nan is reading the back cover. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
A book has two covers, one on each side. The FRONT cover has the title and a picture. Deb is touching the cover on the OTHER side, the one that is not the front.

What is Deb touching?
questions.hint: There are only two covers, and one is the front. If it is not the front cover, what is the other cover called?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The cover on the other side is the back cover. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! One cover is the front. Deb is touching the OTHER one. What is it called?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Deb is touching the back cover. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Name the author and illustrator of a text and define the role of each in presenting the ideas or information
standards.parent_tip: Discuss who wrote the book and who made the pictures, and why someone would write about this topic.

questions.prompt:
A book about dinosaurs says: "Written by Dr. Sara Chen."

Dr. Sara Chen is the...
questions.hint: 'Written by' means this person wrote the words!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The person who writes the book is the author. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! It says 'Written by.' What do we call the person who writes a book?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Dr. Sara Chen is the author. Now you know!

questions.prompt: In an informational book, the ILLUSTRATOR...
choices: Draws pictures and diagrams to help explain
Sells the book at a store
questions.correct: Draws pictures and diagrams to help explain
questions.hint: Pictures and diagrams in a book are made by an artist — what's that artist's job called?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The illustrator draws the pictures and diagrams to help explain. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, the word 'illustrator' sounds like 'illustrate.' What do you think they make?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The illustrator draws pictures and diagrams to help explain. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
A book about space says: "By Astronaut Mike Collins. Photos by NASA."

Who WROTE the words?
questions.hint: 'By' tells you who the author is!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! It says 'By Astronaut Mike Collins,' so he wrote the words. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! NASA took the photos. Who does it say wrote the words, right after 'By'?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Astronaut Mike Collins wrote the words. Now you know!

questions.prompt: In a book about animals, there are real photographs. The person who took the photos is like an...
questions.hint: Photos serve the same purpose as drawings — they show you what things look like!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The person who takes the photos is like an illustrator or photographer. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! The pictures are real photos, not words. Who makes the pictures in a book?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The photo-taker is like an illustrator or photographer. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: A fact book about bugs was made by two people. One person wrote all the true facts about bugs. The other drew clear pictures to help you understand them. Why do informational books need BOTH an author AND an illustrator?
choices: The author writes facts, and the illustrator draws pictures to help explain.
They don't — only stories need both.
The illustrator writes and the author draws.
questions.correct: The author writes facts, and the illustrator draws pictures to help explain.
questions.hint: Words tell you facts, pictures help you see and understand them!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The author writes the facts and the illustrator draws pictures to help. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, one person made the facts and one made the pictures. Do both of those jobs help you?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The author writes facts, and the illustrator draws pictures to help explain. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
A bug book says: "Pictures by Leo. Words by Ana."

In the book, the picture of the ant is missing two of its legs. Who should fix the picture?
questions.hint: Find out whose job is the pictures. The names tell you who did what.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Leo makes the pictures, so Leo should fix the ant picture. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! The problem is with a picture. Who is the one who makes the pictures?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Leo makes the pictures, so Leo should fix it. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Mrs. Diaz made a book all about rain. It is only words. It has no pictures at all, on any page.

Which helper did this book NOT need?
questions.hint: Think about what an illustrator makes. Does this book have any of that?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! With no pictures at all, the book did not need an illustrator. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! This book has only words and no pictures. Which helper makes the pictures?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The book did not need an illustrator, because it had no pictures. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Sam made the funny drawing of a dancing cat. Pat made up the words that tell the cat's story.

Who is the AUTHOR?
questions.hint: The author is the one who makes the words, not the pictures. Match the job to the name.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Pat made up the words, so Pat is the author. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, the author makes the words, not the pictures. Who made up the words?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Pat made the words, so Pat is the author. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
You laughed and laughed at the silly words in a book. You want to make a thank-you card for the person who chose those silly words.

Who should you thank?
choices: The person who drew the cat
The person who colored the pictures
questions.hint: Words are one person's job, pictures are another's. Who makes the words?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The author picks the words, so you should thank the author. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! You loved the silly words, not the drawings. Who makes the words in a book?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! You should thank the author, who chose the silly words. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
A book about the sea has WORDS that tell fish facts. It also has a MAP that shows where the fish live. A map is a kind of picture.

Who made the map?
choices: The person who reads the book
questions.hint: A map is a picture, even though it shows facts. Whose job is pictures?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A map is a picture, so the illustrator made it. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! A map is a kind of picture. Who makes the pictures in a book?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The illustrator made the map, because a map is a picture. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: With prompting and support, describe the relationship between illustrations and the text in which they appear
standards.parent_tip: Point to diagrams or photos and ask 'what is this picture teaching us about?'

questions.prompt:
A book says: "Lions have sharp teeth and big manes." The picture shows a lion with a fluffy mane.

How does the PICTURE help?
choices: It shows what a mane looks like.
questions.correct: It shows what a mane looks like.
questions.hint: If you didn't know what a mane was, the picture would show you!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The picture shows you what a fluffy mane looks like. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, the words say 'mane,' but maybe you can't picture it. What can the picture show you?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The picture shows what a mane looks like. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
A book about the United States has a MAP showing all 50 states.

What does the MAP help you understand?
questions.hint: Maps show WHERE things are!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A map shows you where the states are located. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! A map shows places on it. What does a map of the states help you find?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The map shows where the states are located. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
The text says: "A caterpillar becomes a butterfly." The picture shows egg, caterpillar, cocoon, and butterfly.

How does the PICTURE help?
choices: It shows all the steps in order
It shows something different from the text
questions.correct: It shows all the steps in order
questions.hint: The picture gives MORE details — it shows each step!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The picture shows all the steps in order, from egg to butterfly. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! The picture has an egg, caterpillar, cocoon, AND butterfly. How does it show them?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The picture shows all the steps in order. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
A book about weather has a picture of a thermometer.

Why would the author include this PICTURE?
choices: To help you understand how we measure temperature
Because the book is about thermometers
questions.correct: To help you understand how we measure temperature
questions.hint: Pictures in fact books are there to help explain ideas!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The thermometer picture helps you understand how we measure temperature. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, a thermometer is a tool we use with weather. Why would the author show it to you?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The picture helps you understand how we measure temperature. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
The text says: "Dogs come in many sizes." The picture shows a tiny chihuahua next to a huge Great Dane.

What does the PICTURE help you see?
choices: That all dogs are the same size
That cats are bigger than dogs
How different dog sizes can be
questions.correct: How different dog sizes can be
questions.hint: The picture puts two very different-sized dogs side by side so you can compare!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The picture shows how different dog sizes can be, tiny to huge! Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! One dog is tiny and one is huge in the picture. What does that show you about dogs?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The picture shows how different dog sizes can be. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
A book says: "Frogs live near ponds. Frogs catch bugs to eat." The PICTURE shows a shiny green frog sitting on a lily pad.

What do you learn from the PICTURE that the words did NOT tell you?
choices: That the animal is a frog
questions.hint: Read the words first. Cross out anything the words already told you. What is left can only come from the picture.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The words did not say the color, but the picture shows the frog is green. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, the words already told you about ponds and bugs. What NEW thing does the picture show?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The picture shows that the frog is green. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
A book says: "A baby bird cannot fly yet. Its mother brings it food."

Which picture goes BEST with the SECOND sentence?
choices: An empty nest with no birds in it
A mother bird putting a worm in the baby's mouth
A baby bird sitting all alone in a nest
A bird flying high above the trees
questions.correct: A mother bird putting a worm in the baby's mouth
questions.hint: Point to just the second sentence and picture it in your mind. Which choice matches only that sentence?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The second sentence is about the mother bringing food, so that picture fits best. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! The second sentence says the mother brings it food. Which picture shows that?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The best picture is a mother bird putting a worm in the baby's mouth. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
A book says: "The turtle hides in its shell. Something scared it!" The PICTURE shows a sneaky fox creeping up behind the turtle.

What does the PICTURE tell you that the words do NOT?
questions.hint: The words say something scared the turtle but never say what. Look at the picture to fill in the missing part.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The words say something scared it, but the picture shows the fox that did it. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! The words just say 'something' scared it. What extra thing does the picture show you?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The picture shows what scared the turtle. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
A book says: "An ant is tiny. A dog is much bigger." The author drew ONE picture with a tiny ant standing right next to a big dog.

Why did the author put the ant and the dog in the SAME picture?
choices: To show that the ant is scared of the dog
To show that the ant and dog are friends
To show where the ant and dog live
To show how much bigger the dog is than the ant
questions.correct: To show how much bigger the dog is than the ant
questions.hint: Think about what the words are about. The picture is drawn to help you SEE that same idea.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Putting them side by side shows how much bigger the dog is than the ant. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, one is tiny and one is big, right next to each other. Why put them in the same picture?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! It shows how much bigger the dog is than the ant. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
A book says: "Bees make honey. Bees live in a hive." Next to the words is a DIAGRAM of a bee with little arrows pointing to its wings, its legs, and its stripes.

What does the DIAGRAM help you do that the words do NOT?
choices: Point to the parts of a bee's body
Learn that bees live in a hive
questions.correct: Point to the parts of a bee's body
questions.hint: What is the diagram showing with its arrows? Two choices are things the words already said, so they can't be the diagram's job.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The diagram's little arrows point to the parts of a bee's body. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! The diagram has arrows pointing to wings, legs, and stripes. What do those help you find?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The diagram helps you point to the parts of a bee's body. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: With prompting and support, identify the reasons an author gives to support points in a text
standards.parent_tip: Ask 'why does the author think that?' to practice finding supporting reasons in the text.

questions.prompt:
You should eat vegetables. They have vitamins that keep you healthy and strong.

WHY does the author say you should eat vegetables?
choices: Because they have vitamins that keep you healthy.
questions.correct: Because they have vitamins that keep you healthy.
questions.hint: Look for the REASON the author gives — it comes after the point.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The author says to eat vegetables because they have vitamins that keep you healthy. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about the good stuff inside vegetables. Why does the author say they are good for you?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The author says to eat vegetables because they have vitamins that keep you healthy. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Kids need lots of sleep. Sleep helps your body grow and helps your brain learn new things.

What REASONS does the author give for why kids need sleep?
choices: You need sleep to watch TV.
It helps your body grow and your brain learn.
questions.correct: It helps your body grow and your brain learn.
questions.hint: The second sentence gives you the reasons!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The author says sleep helps your body grow and your brain learn. Those are the two big reasons. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, let's look again. The story tells us what sleep does for kids. Listen for the words about your body and your brain.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Kids need sleep because it helps your body grow and your brain learn. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Helmets are important. They protect your head if you fall off your bike.

What REASON does the author give for wearing a helmet?
choices: They protect your head if you fall.
questions.correct: They protect your head if you fall.
questions.hint: A reason explains WHY something is true or important.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The author says helmets protect your head if you fall. That is the reason to wear one. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Think about why a helmet is important. What does it do for your head when you fall off your bike?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The author says to wear a helmet because it protects your head if you fall. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Based on the story, which reason tells us why dogs make great pets?
questions.hint: Look at the sentences that tell you why dogs make great pets.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Being loyal means dogs stay by your side. That is why they make great pets. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Think about what makes a dog a GREAT friend to you, not just what a dog does each day.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Dogs make great pets because they are loyal. That means they stick with you. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Drinking water is good for you. Water keeps your body working, helps you think clearly, and gives you energy.

Which is NOT a reason the author gives?
questions.hint: Read the passage again — which answer is NOT mentioned?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The author did NOT say water makes you taller. That one is not a real reason. Great catching it!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Three of these ARE reasons the author gave. Find the one that was NOT in the story at all.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The author never said water makes you taller. That is why it was the odd one out. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Wear a hat on sunny days. A hat keeps the hot sun out of your eyes. Wear boots on rainy days. Boots keep your feet dry.

WHY does the author say to wear a hat?
choices: To keep the sun out of your eyes.
questions.correct: To keep the sun out of your eyes.
questions.hint: The story gives a reason for the hat AND a reason for the boots. Find the one that goes with the hat.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The author says a hat keeps the hot sun out of your eyes. That is the reason for a hat. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! The hat part and the boots part do different jobs. Listen again for what the HAT does on a sunny day.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The author says to wear a hat to keep the sun out of your eyes. The boots keep feet dry. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Dogs need to go on walks. Walks let dogs run and play. A dog that stays inside all day gets sad.

WHY does the author think dogs need walks?
choices: So they can meet other dogs.
So they can run, play, and not feel sad.
Because dogs like to smell the grass.
questions.correct: So they can run, play, and not feel sad.
questions.hint: Look at every sentence. Put together what walks let dogs do AND the problem walks help fix.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The author says walks let dogs run and play so they do not feel sad. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! The story tells us how walks make a dog feel. What happens to a dog that stays inside all day?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Dogs need walks so they can run, play, and not feel sad. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
You should plant a garden. A garden gives you fresh food to eat. A garden also gives bees flowers to visit.

WHY does the author say you should plant a garden?
choices: Because gardens need lots of water.
It gives you food and gives bees flowers.
Because gardens are made of dirt.
questions.correct: It gives you food and gives bees flowers.
questions.hint: Some answers are true about gardens but are NOT the reasons the author gives. Pick the reasons that are actually in the story.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The author gives two reasons: a garden gives you food AND gives bees flowers. Great job finding both!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! The story gives TWO good things a garden does. One helps you, and one helps the bees. Listen again.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The author says to plant a garden because it gives you food and gives bees flowers. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Firefighters are helpers. They rush into fires to save people. They carry pets out to keep them safe.

WHY does the author say firefighters are helpers?
choices: They save only cats from trees.
They save people and pets from fires.
questions.correct: They save people and pets from fires.
questions.hint: A reason should EXPLAIN the point, not just say it again. Find the sentence that tells what firefighters do.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The author says firefighters save people and pets from fires. That is why they are helpers. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Think about WHAT firefighters do that makes them helpers. Who do they save from the fire?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Firefighters are helpers because they save people and pets from fires. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Brush your teeth every day. Brushing keeps your teeth clean. Drink milk too. Milk makes your teeth strong.

The author says milk 'makes your teeth strong.' This reason tells you WHY you should —
questions.hint: This story tells you to do two things. Ask: which one does 'makes your teeth strong' belong to?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Strong teeth is the reason to drink milk. That reason goes with milk. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Milk makes your teeth strong. Which thing does that reason tell you to do?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Milk makes teeth strong, so that reason tells you to drink milk. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: With prompting and support, identify basic similarities in and differences between two texts on the same topic
standards.parent_tip: Read two books about the same animal or topic and ask 'what did both books say? What was different?'

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: We read two books about cats. Book 1 told us that cats like to sleep a lot. Book 2 told us that cats like to chase toys. What is one way the books tell us different things about cats?
choices: Both books say cats are soft.
Book 1 says cats are fluffy, but Book 2 says they are playful.
Book 1 says cats like to sleep, but Book 2 says they like to chase toys.
Both books say cats like to sleep a lot.
questions.correct: Book 1 says cats like to sleep, but Book 2 says they like to chase toys.
questions.hint: Look at what each book says cats like to do.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Book 1 said cats sleep and Book 2 said cats chase toys. The books told us different things. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, let's think. What did Book 1 say cats like? What did Book 2 say? Find the answer that matches BOTH books.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Book 1 says cats like to sleep, but Book 2 says they like to chase toys. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Book 1 says: "Rain comes from clouds."
Book 2 says: "Rain helps flowers grow."

How are these books DIFFERENT?
choices: They say the exact same thing.
Book 1 tells WHERE rain comes from; Book 2 tells WHAT rain does.
questions.correct: Book 1 tells WHERE rain comes from; Book 2 tells WHAT rain does.
questions.hint: Both books are about rain, but each tells you something different about it!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Book 1 told WHERE rain comes from, and Book 2 told WHAT rain does. Great job seeing the difference!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Both books ARE about rain, but they tell you different things. What did each book teach you?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Book 1 tells where rain comes from, and Book 2 tells what rain does. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Book 1: "The sun is very hot and gives us light."
Book 2: "The sun helps plants grow by giving them energy."

What do BOTH books agree on?
choices: The sun is important and gives things to Earth.
questions.correct: The sun is important and gives things to Earth.
questions.hint: Both books talk about what the sun gives — light, energy, growth!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Both books say the sun is important and gives things to Earth. That is what they agree on. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Look at both books. One says the sun gives light, one says it gives energy. What do they BOTH show?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Both books agree the sun is important and gives things to Earth. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Book 1: "Butterflies have colorful wings and drink nectar from flowers."
Book 2: "Butterflies start as caterpillars and go through metamorphosis."

What is SIMILAR about both books?
questions.hint: Both books talk about the same animal — even though they share different facts about it.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Both books are all about butterflies. That is what makes them the same. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! One book talks about wings, the other about caterpillars. But what animal are BOTH books about?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Both books are about butterflies, even though they tell different facts. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Book 1 says: "Owls sleep when the sun is up."
Book 2 says: "Owls hunt at night. They fly in the dark."

How are the two books the SAME?
choices: Both books show that owls are awake at night, not in the day.
Both books say that owls cannot see well.
Both books say that owls eat mice.
Both books say that owls sleep at night.
questions.correct: Both books show that owls are awake at night, not in the day.
questions.hint: "Sleep when the sun is up" and "hunt at night" — think about WHEN the owl is busy. Do they point to the same time of day?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Book 1 says owls sleep in the day, Book 2 says they hunt at night. Both show owls are awake at night. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! When does an owl sleep, and when is it busy? Find the idea that fits BOTH books.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Both books show that owls are awake at night, not in the day. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Book 1 says: "Penguins live where it is very cold. They cannot fly."
Book 2 says: "Penguins live where it is cold. They swim very well."

What is ONE way the books are DIFFERENT?
choices: Both books say penguins live where it is hot.
Book 1 says penguins cannot fly; Book 2 says penguins swim well.
Book 1 says penguins are cold; Book 2 says penguins are warm.
Book 1 says penguins can fly; Book 2 says penguins cannot swim.
questions.correct: Book 1 says penguins cannot fly; Book 2 says penguins swim well.
questions.hint: Both books agree it is cold — that part is the SAME. Look past it and find the one thing each book says that the other does not.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Book 1 says penguins cannot fly, and Book 2 says they swim well. That is one way they are different. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, both books say penguins live where it is cold. So that is the same. Look for how the books are DIFFERENT.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Book 1 says penguins cannot fly, and Book 2 says penguins swim well. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Book 1 says: "Bees make honey. We eat the honey."
Book 2 says: "Bees buzz from flower to flower all day."

What do BOTH books help us know about bees?
choices: Both books say that bees like flowers.
Both books say that bees can sting.
Both books show that bees are busy doing a job.
Both books say that bees make honey.
questions.correct: Both books show that bees are busy doing a job.
questions.hint: One book shows bees making honey, the other shows bees flying all day. What is the same about doing those things?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Making honey and buzzing to flowers are both jobs. Both books show bees are busy. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Only one book talks about honey. Look for the idea that goes with BOTH books, not just one.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Both books show that bees are busy doing a job. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Book 1 says: "Trees give us shade. Birds build nests in trees."
Book 2 says: "Birds live in trees. Trees clean our air."

What do BOTH books tell us about trees?
choices: Both books tell us that trees grow apples.
Both books tell us that trees give shade.
Both books tell us that trees clean the air.
Both books tell us that birds are in trees.
questions.correct: Both books tell us that birds are in trees.
questions.hint: Each book says two things. Find the ONE idea that shows up in both, even if the words are different. The shade and the clean air each show up only once.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Book 1 says birds build nests in trees, and Book 2 says birds live in trees. Both talk about birds! Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! One book says trees give shade, one says trees clean air. Those are different. What do BOTH books say?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Both books tell us that birds are in trees. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Follow words from left to right, top to bottom, and page by page
standards.parent_tip: Practice left-to-right tracking by running a finger under words while reading together.

questions.prompt: After you have read all the words on one page of your book, what is the best way to find the next part of the story?
choices: Close the book and pick a new one.
Turn to the next page to continue reading.
Read the same page again from the beginning.
Look for words in the middle of the page you just finished.
questions.correct: Turn to the next page to continue reading.
questions.hint: Think about how we move through a book to keep reading the story.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! When a page is done, you turn to the next page to keep reading. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, the story is not over yet! You read all the words on this page. Where does the story keep going?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! When you finish a page, you turn to the next page to keep reading. Now you know!

questions.prompt: When you finish reading a LINE of words, where do you look next?
choices: Down to the next line on the left side
To the right of the line
questions.correct: Down to the next line on the left side
questions.hint: After you finish a line, your eyes do two things: go DOWN and go BACK to the start.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! When a line is done, you go down to the next line and start on the left. Great reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! You finished the line. Which way do your eyes go, and which side do they start on?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! After a line, you go down to the next line and start on the left side. Now you know!

questions.prompt: When you finish a PAGE, what do you do?
questions.hint: We read page by page — one after another!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! When a page is done, you turn to the next page. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! The book still has more story to read. What do you do so you can keep going?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! When you finish a page, you turn to the next page. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Imagine you are reading these words in a book: The little bird flew to its nest. If you just finished reading the word 'bird' on the first line, which word should you read next?
questions.hint: Think about how your eyes move from the end of one line to the start of the next line when you read.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! We read left to right. After 'bird' comes 'flew.' Great reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! We read from left to right. The word 'bird' is done. Which word sits right next to it?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! After the word 'bird,' the next word is 'flew.' Now you know!

questions.prompt: Where do you START reading on a page?
questions.hint: Reading starts at the very beginning. Where is the beginning of a page — top or bottom? Left or right?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! We start reading at the top left corner. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! Think about where your eyes go first on a page. Is it the top or the bottom? Which side?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! You start reading at the top left corner. Now you know!

questions.prompt: We read words from LEFT to RIGHT. Look at this line: "The cat ran fast." Which word do you read FIRST?
questions.hint: Reading always starts at the very beginning — on the LEFT side of the line!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! We read left to right, so 'The' comes first. Great reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, we read from left to right. Look at the very first word on the left side of the line.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The first word we read is 'The.' Now you know!

questions.prompt: After you finish reading the LAST word on a line, where do your eyes go next?
choices: Skip to the bottom of the page
Down a line, back to the LEFT
Up to the top of the page
questions.correct: Down a line, back to the LEFT
questions.hint: Your eyes drop down one line and go ALL the way back to the start.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! After the last word, your eyes go down a line and back to the left. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! The line is done. Do your eyes go up or down? And which side do they start on?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! After the last word, your eyes go down a line and back to the left. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Read this sentence. It is written on two lines:
"Sam has a pet.
 We like him."
You just finished reading "pet" at the end of the first line. Which word do you read NEXT?
questions.hint: At the end of a line, your eyes sweep DOWN and all the way back to the far LEFT to start the next line.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! After 'pet' ends the top line, you drop down and start the next line with 'We.' Great reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! You finished the first line at 'pet.' Now go down to the new line. What is its very first word?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! After 'pet,' you go to the next line and read 'We' first. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
This sentence is written on two lines:
"A fox can run
 very fast today."
Which word do you read LAST?
questions.hint: Reading a page ends at the very bottom, on the far right side of the last line.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! You read the last word on the last line, and that is 'today.' Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! The LAST word is at the very end of the bottom line. Which word is all the way at the end?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The last word you read is 'today.' Now you know!

questions.prompt: You open your book to a brand-new page that is full of words. Which word do you read FIRST?
choices: The word at the bottom on the left
The biggest word on the page
The word at the top on the left
The word at the top on the right
questions.correct: The word at the top on the left
questions.hint: Words start at the top of the page, and each line starts on the left side.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! On a new page, you start at the top on the left. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, it is not the biggest word or the bottom word. Think about where reading always begins. Top or bottom? Which side?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! You read the word at the top on the left first. Now you know!

questions.prompt: A page has three short lines of words. You just finished reading the TOP line. Which line do you read next, and where do you start it?
choices: The top line again, starting on the LEFT
The middle line, starting on the RIGHT
The bottom line, starting on the LEFT
The middle line, starting on the LEFT
questions.correct: The middle line, starting on the LEFT
questions.hint: Go straight down to the very next line, then jump back to its far left before you read.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! After the top line, you go to the middle line and start on the left. Great reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! You finished the top line. You move down one line, not skip. And which side do you start on?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! You read the middle line next, starting on the left. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Here is a word: w - a - s. To read it the correct way (was), which letter do you say FIRST?
questions.hint: Always read the letters left to right, starting at the very first letter on the left.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! We say letters left to right, so 'w' comes first in was. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! We read letters from left to right. Which letter is first, all the way on the left?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The first letter you say is 'w.' Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Recognize that spoken words are represented in written language by specific sequences of letters
standards.parent_tip: Point to words and say 'these letters make this word' — connect spoken words to print.

questions.prompt: Words are made up of...
questions.hint: A, B, C, D... we use these to build words!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Words are made up of letters. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about what you see when you spell a word like cat. Is it colors, or is it something else?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Words are made up of letters. Now you know!

questions.prompt: If you change the letters in a word, what happens?
choices: You get a different word or a nonsense word
questions.correct: You get a different word or a nonsense word
questions.hint: C-A-T is 'cat' but C-A-P is 'cap' — different letters make different words!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Change the letters and you get a different word or a nonsense word. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! If you swap letters in a word, does it stay the same, or does it turn into something new?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! When you change the letters, you get a different word or a nonsense word. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Every word we say can also be...
questions.hint: We can write down anything we say using letters!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Every word we say can also be written with letters. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Think about how we put a spoken word on paper. What do we use to write it down?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Every word we say can also be written with letters. Now you know!

questions.prompt: The letters d, o, and g can spell the word 'dog.' Which row shows the letters in the RIGHT order to spell 'dog'?
questions.hint: Say the word slowly and match each sound to its letter, left to right. All rows have the same letters, so the ORDER is what matters.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The letters in the right order are d-o-g. Great spelling!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Say the word dog slowly. Which sound do you hear first, then next, then last? Match the letter order.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The right order to spell dog is d-o-g. Now you know!

questions.prompt: The words 'pan' and 'nap' are built from the very same three letters: p, a, n. Why are they two DIFFERENT words?
choices: They are really the same word
The letters are in a different order
One word has different letters than the other
One word is longer than the other
questions.correct: The letters are in a different order
questions.hint: Look closely at both words. Do they use different letters, or the same letters arranged differently?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Pan and nap use the same letters, but in a different order. That makes two words. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! Both words use p, a, and n. Look closely. What is not the same about how the letters are lined up?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Pan and nap are different because the letters are in a different order. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which pair of words is made from the SAME three letters, just put in a different order?
questions.hint: Check if BOTH words use the exact same letters. In some pairs one letter is swapped for a new one instead of reordered.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Tap and pat both use t, a, and p, just moved around. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, check each pair. You want two words that share the SAME three letters, only mixed up. Which pair matches?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Tap and pat are made from the same three letters in a different order. Now you know!

questions.prompt: The word 'bat' is spelled b-a-t. Which ONE letter would you change to make it spell 'bit'?
questions.hint: Line the two words up letter by letter: b-a-t and b-i-t. Which spot is different?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Change the middle letter, a to i, and bat becomes bit. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Say bat, then bit. The first sound and last sound stay the same. Which sound in the middle changed?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! You change the middle letter, a to i, to make bit. Now you know!

questions.prompt: You hear the word 'pin.' Which row of letters spells the word 'pin'?
questions.hint: Stretch the word: /p/ /i/ /n/. Check the first sound, middle sound, AND last sound against each row.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The word pin is spelled p-i-n. Great listening and spelling!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Say pin slowly and listen to each sound. Which row of letters matches every sound you hear?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The word pin is spelled p-i-n. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Understand that words are separated by spaces in print
standards.parent_tip: Point out spaces between words on a page — 'see how each word has a little gap?'

questions.prompt:
In print, each word has a space around it. Count the words in this line:

the big cat sat on a mat
questions.hint: Touch each word one at a time. Every space starts a new word. Don't stop early!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! There are 7 words, because each one has a space around it. Great counting!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Point to each word and count them one at a time. The spaces help you tell where each word ends.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The line has 7 words. Count each one with a space around it. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Spaces go BETWEEN the words. How many spaces are between the words in this line?

red hot sun
questions.hint: Count the words first, then the gaps. There is always one less gap than there are words.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! There are 3 words, so there are 2 spaces between them. Great counting!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Spaces go BETWEEN words, not on the ends. Count the gaps in red hot sun. How many gaps do you see?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! There are 2 spaces between the words. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which line puts a space between every word so we can tell the words apart?
questions.hint: Read each choice slowly. A space should come exactly where one whole word ends and the next begins.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'i see the sun' has a space between every word, so we can read them all. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! We need one space between EVERY word. Find the line where no words are stuck together.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The line 'i see the sun' has a space between every word. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
A space is missing in this line. Where should the space go to make TWO real words?

sundog
questions.hint: Say the line out loud and listen for two little words you know. Break it where one word ends.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Put the space as 'sun dog' to make two real words. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, try saying the parts out loud. Where can you split it so BOTH pieces are real words you know?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The space goes to make 'sun dog,' two real words. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Only one of these lines has exactly 4 words. Which one is it?
questions.hint: Count the words in each line by tapping every space. Look for the line that lands on 4.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'a big red dog' has exactly 4 words. Great counting!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Count the words in each line one by one. You want the line with exactly 4 words.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The line with 4 words is 'a big red dog.' Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Recognize and name all upper- and lowercase letters of the alphabet
standards.parent_tip: Practice letter names with alphabet puzzles, fridge magnets, or writing letters in sand.

questions.prompt: Which is the UPPERCASE (capital) version of the letter "a"?
questions.hint: Uppercase letters are BIG letters — the capital version of 'a' is...
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Big A is the uppercase, or capital, version of little a. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Little a and its big letter make the same sound. Which BIG letter goes with little a?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The uppercase version of a is A. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which is the LOWERCASE version of the letter "G"?
questions.hint: Lowercase letters are small letters. The small 'G' looks like 'g.'
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Little g is the lowercase version of big G. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Big G and its little letter make the same sound. Which small letter goes with big G?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The lowercase version of G is g. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which of these letters is the letter P?
questions.hint: Look carefully at each letter. Which one has a straight line and a curved part on top that touches the line?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! That is the letter P. Great job spotting it!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Listen for the puh sound. That letter has a straight line with one bump near the top. Which one is it?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The letter P is the right one. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which choice shows the uppercase letter P?
questions.hint: Look carefully at the shape of each letter to find the one that looks like P.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! That is the big letter P. It stands up tall with a bump at the top. Great looking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, look again! You want the tall letter with a straight line and one little bump near the top. Which choice is it?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The uppercase P is the letter with a straight line and one bump up top. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Look carefully! Which one of these is the lowercase letter "d"?
questions.hint: The letter d has its round belly on the LEFT side and the tall stick on the right — trace the circle first, then find where the line stands.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! That is the little letter d. It has a circle first, then a tall line. Great looking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Look for the letter with its round part on the LEFT and a tall line on the RIGHT. Which one is that?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The lowercase d has the circle on the left and the tall line on the right. Now you know!

questions.prompt: This capital letter is "G". Which lowercase letter goes with it?
questions.hint: Say the letter names out loud. Which little letter is named "gee"? All of these dip below the line, so listen for the NAME, not just the tail.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Big G and little g are a team. Great matching!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! The little partner for this capital starts the word go and the word goat. Which lowercase letter is it?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The lowercase g goes with capital G. They make the same sound. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which one shows a CAPITAL letter matched with its correct lowercase partner?
questions.hint: Check each pair by saying both letters' names. Only ONE pair says the SAME name twice (like "Are, are").
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Big R and little r match up. They are a real pair. Great looking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Look for a big letter and its little letter that look like a family. Which pair goes together?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! R and r is the pair that matches. Big R and little r are a team. Now you know!

questions.prompt: This lowercase letter is "r". Which CAPITAL letter matches it?
questions.hint: The lowercase r and capital R don't look much alike, so say the letter name "arr" and pick the big letter with the same name.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Little r and big R are a team. Great matching!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! You need the BIG partner for this little letter. It has the same name. Which capital is it?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The capital R matches the little r. They go together. Now you know!

questions.prompt: This lowercase letter is "w". Which CAPITAL letter matches it?
questions.hint: Watch out — one choice is just "w" turned upside down! Say the name "double-u" and pick the matching big letter.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Little w and big W both look like waves. Great matching!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! You need the BIG partner for this little letter. It has the same pointy, wavy shape. Which capital is it?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The capital W matches little w. They both look like waves. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Recognize and produce rhyming words
standards.parent_tip: Play rhyming games — 'what rhymes with cat? Hat, bat, mat!' Do this during car rides or walks.

questions.prompt: Which pair of words rhymes?
questions.hint: Focus on the ending sounds of the words in each pair.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Those words end with the same sound, so they rhyme. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, say each pair out loud. Rhyming words end with the same sound. Which two sound alike at the end?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Fan and Can rhyme. They both end with the -an sound. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Three of these words rhyme. Which word does NOT rhyme with the others?
cat, hat, hot, bat
questions.hint: Say each word slowly and listen to the ending sound. Three of them end the same way — find the one that ends differently.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! That word does not sound like the others at the end. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Three words end with the -at sound. Say them slowly. Which one has a different ending?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Hot is the one that does not rhyme. Cat, hat, and bat all end in -at. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Which word rhymes with 'chair'?
bear, chin, cheer, chip
questions.hint: Listen for the ending sound in 'chair.' Some words that start like 'chair' do NOT rhyme with it — pick the one whose ending sound matches.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! That word ends with the same sound as chair. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Say chair out loud. Which word ends with that same -air sound?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Bear rhymes with chair. They both end with the -air sound. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Which word rhymes with 'sun'?
sit, sad, bun, sock
questions.hint: Rhymes match the ENDING sound, not the beginning. Say each word and listen to how it ends.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! That word ends with the same sound as sun. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! Say sun slowly. Which word ends with that same -un sound?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Bun rhymes with sun. They both end with the -un sound. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Which word rhymes with 'cake'?
cane, lake, cape, cat
questions.hint: Say 'cake' and stretch its ending. Look for the word that ends with that same '-ake' sound.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! That word ends with the same sound as cake. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Say cake out loud. Which word ends with that same -ake sound?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Lake rhymes with cake. They both end with the -ake sound. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Which word rhymes with 'ten'?
tan, tin, hen, top
questions.hint: Focus on the middle and ending sounds. A tiny change in the vowel can break a rhyme, so listen closely.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! That word ends with the same sound as ten. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Say ten slowly. Which word ends with that same -en sound?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Hen rhymes with ten. They both end with the -en sound. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Count, pronounce, blend, and segment syllables in spoken words
standards.parent_tip: Clap out syllables in everyday words — 'wa-ter' is 2 claps, 'el-e-phant' is 3!

questions.prompt: How many SYLLABLES (claps) are in the word "cat"?
questions.hint: Say it and clap: CAT. Just one clap!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Cat is just one clap. Great counting!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, clap the word cat with me. How many claps did you feel?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Cat has 1 syllable. You clap it just one time. Now you know!

questions.prompt: How many SYLLABLES (claps) are in the word "banana"?
questions.hint: Clap it out: ba-NA-na. That's 3 claps!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Ba-na-na is three claps. Great counting!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Clap the word banana slowly, one clap for each part. How many claps?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Banana has 3 syllables: ba-na-na. Three claps! Now you know!

questions.prompt: How many SYLLABLES are in the word "butterfly"?
questions.hint: Clap it: BUT-ter-fly. Count the claps!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! But-ter-fly is three claps. Great counting!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Clap the word butterfly slowly. Say each part and count your claps. How many?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Butterfly has 3 syllables: but-ter-fly. Three claps! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word has 2 SYLLABLES?
questions.hint: Clap each word: dog (1), ap-ple (2), cat (1), fish (1).
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Ap-ple is two claps. Great counting!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Clap each word. You want the one with two claps. Which word has two parts?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Apple has 2 syllables: ap-ple. Two claps! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Put these syllables together: "rain" + "bow." What word is it?
questions.hint: Blend the parts together: rain + bow = ?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Rain and bow snap together to make one word. Great blending!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, say rain, then say bow, right after each other. What word do they make?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Rain plus bow makes Rainbow. Now you know!

questions.prompt: How many SYLLABLES (claps) are in the word "kite"?
questions.hint: Say the word slowly and feel your chin drop. The 'e' at the end is silent — it does not make its own clap.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Kite is just one clap. Great counting!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! Clap the word kite with me. How many claps did you feel?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Kite has 1 syllable. You clap it one time. Now you know!

questions.prompt: How many SYLLABLES (claps) are in the word "turtle"?
questions.hint: Clap it out: tur...tle. The '-le' chunk hides a beat even though you can't hear a clear vowel.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Tur-tle is two claps. Great counting!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Clap the word turtle slowly. Say each part. How many claps?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Turtle has 2 syllables: tur-tle. Two claps! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word has ONLY ONE syllable (one clap)?
questions.hint: Clap each word out loud. Three of them break into two beats — find the one that stays a single clap.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! That word is just one clap. Great counting!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Clap each word. You want the one with only ONE clap. Which one is it?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Snake has only one syllable. You clap it one time. Now you know!

questions.prompt: How many SYLLABLES (claps) are in the word "jumped"?
questions.hint: Say it the way you talk, not letter by letter. The '-ed' just adds a /t/ sound here — no new clap.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Jumped is just one clap. Great counting!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Say jumped out loud. It sounds long, but clap it. How many claps?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Jumped has 1 syllable. Even the -ed is part of one clap. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word has TWO syllables (two claps)?
questions.hint: Clap each word. Watch out — a silent 'e' can make a short word LOOK longer than it sounds.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! That word has two claps. Great counting!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Clap each word. You want the one with TWO claps. Which word has two parts?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Pencil has two syllables: pen-cil. Two claps! Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Blend and segment onsets and rimes of single-syllable spoken words
standards.parent_tip: Break words into parts: 'cat' = /c/ + /at/. Try with other words like bat, sit, fun.

questions.prompt: Blend the onset and rime together: /f/ ... 'ish'. What word do you hear?
questions.hint: Say the first sound, then hold the ending part 'ish', and slide them together slowly.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! /f/ and 'ish' snap together. Great blending!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, say /f/ then 'ish' right together. What word do you hear?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! /f/ and 'ish' make the word fish. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Blend these parts into one word: /st/ ... 'op'. What word do they make?
questions.hint: The first part has TWO sounds blended, /s/ then /t/. Say them, then add 'op'.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! /st/ and 'op' snap together. Great blending!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Say /st/ then 'op' right together, nice and fast. What word do they make?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! /st/ and 'op' make the word stop. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Say the word 'clap' in two parts: the beginning sounds and the rest. Which shows the correct onset and rime?
questions.hint: The onset is ALL the sounds before the first vowel. Find the vowel in 'clap', then split right before it.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Clap breaks into /cl/ and 'ap'. Great sound splitting!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Say clap slowly. The first sounds go together, then the rest. Which one splits it right?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Clap splits into /cl/ and 'ap'. That is the beginning and the rest. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Blend the onset and rime: /ch/ ... 'in'. What word do you hear?
questions.hint: The first sound is one sound made by two letters: /ch/. Say it, then blend on 'in'.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! /ch/ and 'in' snap together. Great blending!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! Say /ch/ then 'in' right together. What word do you hear?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! /ch/ and 'in' make the word chin. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Put the onset and rime together: /g/ ... 'ift'. What word do they make?
questions.hint: Hold the ending part 'ift' — it has three sounds. Add /g/ to the front and blend it all.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! /g/ and 'ift' snap together. Great blending!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Say /g/ then 'ift' right together. What word do they make?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! /g/ and 'ift' make the word gift. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Isolate and pronounce the initial, medial vowel, and final sounds (phonemes) in three-phoneme (CVC) words
standards.parent_tip: Play 'first sound, last sound' — ask what sound a word starts or ends with during daily activities.

questions.prompt: Which word has the /i/ sound in the middle?
questions.hint: Listen carefully to the sound in the middle of each word.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! That word has the /i/ sound right in the middle. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, say each word slowly and listen to the middle sound. Which one has an /i/ in the middle?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Pig has the /i/ sound in the middle. Now you know!

questions.prompt: What is the MIDDLE sound in the word "pig"?
questions.hint: P - I - G. The middle sound is the vowel!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The middle sound in pig is /i/. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Say pig slowly and stretch out the middle part. What sound do you hear between the first and last?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The middle sound in pig is /i/. Now you know!

questions.prompt: What is the FIRST sound in the word "fun"?
questions.hint: Say 'fun' slowly. The very first sound is...
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Fun starts with the /f/ sound. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Say fun out loud. Listen to the very first sound your mouth makes. What is it?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The first sound in fun is /f/. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
The word "bed" has 3 sounds.

What is the MIDDLE sound?
questions.hint: The middle sound is always the vowel — the one in between!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The middle sound in bed is /e/. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Say bed slowly and stretch the middle. What vowel sound do you hear between the b and the d?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The middle sound in bed is /e/. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Say the word "web" slowly: /w/ /e/ /b/. Which word has the SAME middle sound as "web"?
questions.hint: Stretch each word and listen only to the vowel sound in the middle, not the beginning or end.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! That word has the same /e/ middle sound as web. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, web has an /e/ in the middle. Say each word and find the one with that same middle sound.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Ten has the same middle sound as web, the /e/ sound. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word ends with the /k/ sound at the very end?
questions.hint: Listen to the LAST sound only. The 'ck' spelling makes one /k/ sound.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! That word ends with the /k/ sound. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Say each word and listen to the very last sound. Which one ends with /k/?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Duck ends with the /k/ sound. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Say "cot" and "cut." They share the first sound /k/ and the last sound /t/. Which word has the SAME middle sound as "cut"?
questions.hint: Focus on the vowel in the very middle of the word, and match it to the /u/ you hear inside "cut."
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! That word has the same /u/ middle sound as cut. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Cut has a /u/ in the middle. Say each word and find the one with that same middle sound.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Mud has the same middle sound as cut, the /u/ sound. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word has the /f/ sound at the BEGINNING?
questions.hint: Say the first sound out loud. Ask: does it start with the /f/ sound, even if it isn't spelled with an f?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! That word starts with the /f/ sound, even with a ph! Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Say each word out loud. Two might trick you. Which one really begins with the /f/ sound?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Phone begins with the /f/ sound. The letters ph say /f/. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word has the /o/ sound (like in "hot") in the MIDDLE?
questions.hint: All four start with /j/. Ignore the first sound and listen to just the vowel in the middle.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! That word has the /o/ sound in the middle, like hot. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! Say each word slowly. Which one has the /o/ sound, like in hot, in the middle?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Job has the /o/ middle sound, just like hot. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Add or substitute individual sounds (phonemes) in simple, one-syllable words to make new words
standards.parent_tip: Try word swap games: 'If we change the /c/ in cat to /b/, what word do we get? Bat!'

questions.prompt: Let's play with sounds! Say the word 'map'. Now, change the very first sound, /m/, to the /t/ sound. What new word do you hear?
questions.hint: Listen carefully to the beginning sound you are asked to change in 'map' and then what new sound you should make.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Swap /m/ for /t/ and map becomes that word. Great sound swapping!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, take map and put /t/ at the front instead of /m/. Blend it slow. What word do you hear?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Change /m/ to /t/ in map and you get tap. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Look at these pairs of words. In which pair was a new word made by changing only one sound?
questions.hint: Think about which two words sound almost the same, but have just one sound different.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Those two words are the same except for one sound. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Look for two words that sound almost the same, with just one sound changed. Which pair?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Cat and hat change only one sound, the first one. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Listen to the word 'dog'. If you change the middle sound, /o/, to the /i/ sound, what new word do you make?
questions.hint: Focus on the sound in the middle of 'dog' and how it changes to the /i/ sound.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Swap the /o/ for /i/ and dog becomes that word. Great sound swapping!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Take dog and change the middle /o/ to /i/. Blend it slow. What new word do you hear?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Change the /o/ in dog to /i/ and you get Dig. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Say the word 'pin'. Now change the MIDDLE sound, /i/, to the /a/ sound. What new word do you hear?
questions.hint: Keep the first sound /p/ and the last sound /n/ the same. Only the middle vowel changes.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Swap the /i/ for /a/ and pin becomes that word. Great sound swapping!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Take pin and change the middle /i/ to /a/. Blend it slow. What word do you hear?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Change the /i/ in pin to /a/ and you get pan. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Say the word 'bat'. Now change the LAST sound, /t/, to the /g/ sound. What new word do you hear?
questions.hint: Keep 'ba-' at the start and only change the ending sound.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Swap the /t/ for /g/ and bat becomes that word. Great sound swapping!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Take bat and change the last /t/ to /g/. Blend it slow. What new word do you hear?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Change the last /t/ in bat to /g/ and you get bag. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Say the word 'lip'. Now ADD the /s/ sound to the very front. What new word do you hear?
questions.hint: Put the new /s/ sound before the rest of the word, then blend it all together.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Add /s/ to the front of lip and you get that word. Great sound adding!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, put an /s/ sound right in front of lip. Blend it slow. What new word do you hear?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Add /s/ to the front of lip and you get slip. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Say the word 'cot'. Now change the FIRST sound, /k/, to the /h/ sound. What new word do you hear?
questions.hint: Keep the '-ot' ending and only change the beginning sound.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Swap the /k/ for /h/ and cot becomes that word. Great sound swapping!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Take cot and change the first /k/ to /h/. Blend it slow. What new word do you hear?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Change the first sound in cot to /h/ and you get hot. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Look at these pairs. In which pair is the new word made by changing ONLY the middle sound?
questions.hint: Check that the first sound and the last sound stay the same, and only the vowel in the middle changes.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! In that pair only the middle sound changed. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Not yet! Look for the pair where the first and last sounds stay the same and only the middle changes.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! In hop, hip only the middle sound changed. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Demonstrate basic knowledge of one-to-one letter-sound correspondences by producing the primary sound for each consonant
standards.parent_tip: Practice letter sounds with flashcards or apps — 'B says /b/ like in ball!'

questions.prompt: The letter B makes the /b/ sound. Which word starts with the /b/ sound?
questions.hint: Try each word out loud. Only one begins by pressing your lips together to make /b/.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! That word starts with the /b/ sound, like the letter B. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, say each word out loud. Which one begins with the /b/ sound, like B?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Bat starts with the /b/ sound. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word begins with the SAME first sound as the word "sun"?
questions.hint: Say "sun" and stretch out its very first sound. Now find the word that starts the same way.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! "Sock" starts with the /s/ sound, just like "sun." Ssss-sock, ssss-sun. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, say each word slow. Which one starts with that ssss sound at the front, like "sun"? Try again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! "Sun" and "sock" both start with ssss. Say them slow: ssss-un, ssss-ock. Now you know!

questions.prompt: What is the very first sound you hear in the word "dog"?
questions.hint: Say "dog" slowly and stop right at the beginning. What sound does your tongue make first?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! "Dog" starts with the /d/ sound. D-d-dog. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Say "dog" nice and slow. What is the very first little sound your mouth makes? Try again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The first sound in "dog" is /d/. D-d-dog. Now you know!

questions.prompt: The word "cat" starts with the /k/ sound. Which letter makes that /k/ sound at the start of "cat"?
questions.hint: Look at how the word "cat" is spelled. Which letter is at the very front?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The letter "c" makes the /k/ sound at the start of "cat." Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Think about which letter you see at the very start of "cat." Take another look!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The letter "c" makes the /k/ sound in "cat." Now you know!

questions.prompt: The word "gate" starts with the /g/ sound (like "go"). Which word below starts with the SAME /g/ sound as "gate"?
questions.hint: Say each word slowly. Listen for the hard /g/ sound at the very start, like in "go" — not a soft /j/ sound.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! "Gum" starts with the same /g/ sound as "gate." G-g-gum, g-g-gate. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, say each word slow. Some make a soft /j/ sound. Which one starts with the hard /g/ like "gate"? Try again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! "Gum" starts with the /g/ sound, just like "gate." G-g-gum. Now you know!

questions.prompt: The word "kite" starts with the /k/ sound. One of these words ALSO starts with the /k/ sound but is spelled with a C. Which one?
questions.hint: Two letters can make /k/. Say each word — "city" starts with C but says /s/. Which one truly begins with /k/?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! "Cup" starts with the /k/ sound but uses the letter C. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Say each word slow. Which one makes the /k/ sound like "kite" but starts with a C? Try again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! "Cup" makes the /k/ sound with a C at the front. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Say these words out loud: "fish," "fun," "vase," "fan." Which word does NOT start with the /f/ sound?
questions.hint: Put your top teeth on your bottom lip. /f/ is a quiet, breathy sound; /v/ buzzes. Which word buzzes instead?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! "Vase" starts with a /v/ sound, not /f/. Fish, fun, and fan all buzz with /f/. Great ears!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Say each word slow. Three make the /f/ sound. Which one starts a little different? Try again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! "Vase" starts with /v/, not /f/. Vvv-ase. Now you know!

questions.prompt: The word "win" starts with the /w/ sound. Which word below starts with the SAME /w/ sound as "win"?
questions.hint: Say each word slowly. Listen for the /w/ sound made by your lips, and don't confuse /w/ with /v/ or /y/.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! "Wheel" starts with the same /w/ sound as "win." W-w-wheel. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Say each word slow. Which one starts with that /w/ sound like "win"? Take another look!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! "Wheel" starts with the /w/ sound, just like "win." Now you know!

questions.prompt: Say these words: "jam," "gem," "dog," "gum." Which word does NOT start with the /g/ sound (like in "go")?
questions.hint: Careful — the question asks which does NOT start with /g/. Some of these start with /j/ or /d/, and G can trick you by saying /j/.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! "Gum" was the word we were listening for. Great job using your ears!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, say each word slow and listen to the very first sound. Which one is the odd one out? Try again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The word we wanted was "gum." Say it slow: g-um. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Associate the long and short sounds with the common spellings (graphemes) for the five major vowels
standards.parent_tip: Compare short and long vowels: 'cap has a short a, cape has a long a — hear the difference?'

questions.prompt: The word "cake" has a long 'a' sound, and "cat" has a short 'a' sound. Now, look at the word "rake". What kind of 'a' sound do you hear in "rake"?
questions.hint: Remember that the silent 'e' at the end of a word can make the vowel before it say its long sound.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! "Rake" has a long 'a' sound, just like "cake." It says its own name, A! Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, say "rake" out loud. Does the 'a' say its name, or make a short sound? Try again!
