questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! "Rake" has a long 'a' sound, like in "cake." It says A! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word has a SHORT **i** sound (like in "pig")?
questions.hint: Say each word slowly. Which one has the same middle sound as 'pig'?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! "Sit" has a short i sound, just like "pig." I-i-sit. Great ears!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Say each word slow. Which one has that short i sound like "pig"? Take another look!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! "Sit" has the short i sound, like in "pig." Now you know!

questions.prompt: Three of these words have a LONG **o** sound. Which word has a DIFFERENT vowel sound?
questions.hint: Listen carefully to the sound of the letter 'o' in each word. Does it sound like the 'o' in 'note' (long O) or the 'o' in 'dog' (short O)?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! "Fox" has a short o sound. Rope, home, and bone all have the long o. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Say each word slow. Three sound alike in the middle. Which one is different? Try again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! "Fox" has a short o sound, so it was the different one. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word has a SHORT **e** sound (like in "bed")?
questions.hint: Say each word slowly. Which one has the same middle sound as 'bed'?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! "Red" has a short e sound, just like "bed." E-e-red. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, say each word slow. Which one has that short e sound like "bed"? Try again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! "Red" has the short e sound, like in "bed." Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word has a SHORT **u** sound (like in "bus")?
questions.hint: Say each word slowly. Which one has the same middle sound as 'bus'?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! "Cup" has a short u sound, just like "bus." U-u-cup. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Say each word slow. Which one has that short u sound like "bus"? Take another look!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! "Cup" has the short u sound, like in "bus." Now you know!

questions.prompt: All of these words have a magic 'e' at the end. Three of them make the vowel say its LONG name. Which word does NOT have a long vowel sound? "cane", "cone", "have", "cube"
questions.hint: Try saying each word out loud. Does the vowel say its own name (long) or a quick sound (short)? Listen for the odd one out.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! "Have" does not say a long vowel, even with the e. Cane, cone, and cube all say their name. Great ears!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, say each word slow. Three say their vowel name. Which one does not? Try again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! "Have" keeps a short sound, so it was the odd one. Now you know!

questions.prompt: The word "go" has a long 'o' sound. Which of these words also has the LONG 'o' sound? "got", "no", "box", "fox"
questions.hint: Say each word slowly. Does the 'o' say its name like in 'go', or make the short sound like in 'hot'?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! "No" has the long 'o' sound, just like "go." It says its name, O! Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Say each word slow. Which one has the long 'o' that says its name, like "go"? Try again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! "No" has the long 'o' sound, like "go." Now you know!

questions.prompt: Two words below use a DIFFERENT spelling but make the very same long 'e' sound as in "me". Which word makes the LONG 'e' sound? "bed", "see", "pet", "ten"
questions.hint: The letters 'ee' can spell the long 'e' sound. Say each word and listen for the one that says the letter name 'e'.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! "See" makes the long 'e' sound, just like "me." Eee! Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Say each word slow. Which one makes the long 'e' sound like "me"? Take another look!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! "See" makes the long 'e' sound, like "me." Now you know!

questions.prompt: The word "cut" has a short 'u' sound. Which of these words has a LONG 'u' sound (like in "cute")? "cub", "mule", "bug", "sun"
questions.hint: Add the magic-e test: does the vowel say its name? Sound out each word carefully.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! "Mule" has the long 'u' sound, just like "cute." It says its name, U! Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, say each word slow. Which one has the long 'u' that says its name, like "cute"? Try again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! "Mule" has the long 'u' sound, like "cute." Now you know!

questions.prompt: Three of these words have a SHORT vowel sound. Which word has a LONG vowel sound? "rope", "top", "mop", "hop"
questions.hint: Look for the silent 'e' at the end, then check if the vowel says its name.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! "Rope" has a long vowel that says its name, O. Top, mop, and hop are short. Great ears!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Say each word slow. Three are short. Which one has a long vowel that says its name? Try again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! "Rope" has the long vowel sound, so it was the different one. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Read common high-frequency words by sight (e.g., the, of, to, you, she, my, is, are, do, does)
standards.parent_tip: Practice sight words with flashcards during short daily sessions — even 5 minutes helps!

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: 'The dog ___ big.' Which word fits in the blank?
questions.hint: One dog is just one. Which little word do we use when we talk about only one thing?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! "The dog is big" sounds just right. One dog uses "is." Great reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, read it out loud. There's just one dog. Which little word sounds right when you say it? Try again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! "The dog is big" is correct. One thing uses "is." Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: 'The cats ___ big.' Which word fits in the blank?
questions.hint: 'Cats' means more than one. Which word do we use when we talk about many things?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! "The cats are big" sounds just right. More than one uses "are." Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Read it out loud. There is more than one cat. Which word sounds right? Take another look!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! "The cats are big" is correct. More than one uses "are." Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: 'What ___ you see?' Which word fits in the blank?
questions.hint: Say it out loud with 'you'. Do we say 'do you' or 'does you'?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! "What do you see?" sounds just right. Great reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, read it out loud with each word. Which one makes the question sound right? Try again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! "What do you see?" is correct. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: 'What ___ she want?' Which word fits in the blank?
questions.hint: Say it with 'she'. Do we say 'she do' or 'she does'?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! "What does she want?" sounds just right. With "she" we use "does." Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Read it out loud. The word "she" needs a special helper word. Which one sounds right? Try again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! "What does she want?" is correct. With "she" we use "does." Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word is 'she'? Point to the one that says 'she'.
questions.hint: Look carefully at the first letters and the whole word. 'She' starts with s-h.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! You pointed to "she." Great reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Look at each word carefully. Which one spells the word we want? Take another look!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The word "she" was the right one. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Distinguish between similarly spelled words by identifying the sounds of the letters that differ
standards.parent_tip: Put similar words side by side — 'cat vs cut — what letter changed? What sound changed?'

questions.prompt: Listen to the words MAP and MOP. Which sound changes in the middle of these words?
questions.hint: Listen carefully to the sound in the middle of MAP and then the sound in the middle of MOP.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The middle sound changes from /a/ to /o/. Map, mop! Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, say "map" then "mop." The first and last sounds stay the same. What changes in the middle? Try again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The middle sound changes from /a/ to /o/. Map, mop! Now you know!

questions.prompt: If you change the **D** in "dog" to **L**, what new word do you get?
questions.hint: D-O-G → L-O-G!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Change the /d/ to /l/ and "dog" becomes "log." Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Say it slow. Take off the /d/ and put an /l/ at the front. What word do you hear? Try again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! "Dog" with an /l/ becomes "log." Now you know!

questions.prompt: Listen to the words PET and PIT. Which sound is different in these two words?
questions.hint: Say both words slowly. Two of the three sounds are the same. Listen hard to the middle sound in each word.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The middle sound changes from /e/ to /i/. Pet, pit! Great ears!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, say "pet" then "pit." The first and last sounds match. Listen to the middle. Try again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The middle sound changes, /e/ to /i/. Pet, pit! Now you know!

questions.prompt: The word is BUS. If you change the last sound to make BUG, which sound did you change?
questions.hint: The beginning /b/ and the middle /u/ stay the same in both words. Listen to the very end of each word.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! You changed the last sound, /s/ to /g/, to make "bug." Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Say "bus" then "bug." The start stays the same. Which sound changed at the end? Try again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The last sound changed from /s/ to /g/. Bus, bug! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Listen to CAP and CAB. Which sound changes between these two words?
questions.hint: The first two sounds match. Put your hand on your throat and say /p/ then /b/ — one makes your throat buzz.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The last sound changes from /p/ to /b/. Cap, cab! Great ears!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Say "cap" then "cab." The start matches. Listen to the very last sound. Try again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The last sound changes, /p/ to /b/. Cap, cab! Now you know!

questions.prompt: The word is TEN. If you change the FIRST sound to make HEN, which sound did you change?
questions.hint: Say TEN, then say HEN. The -en part stays exactly the same. Only listen to the very first sound.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! You changed the first sound, /t/ to /h/, to make "hen." Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, say "ten" then "hen." The end stays the same. Which sound changed at the front? Try again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The first sound changed from /t/ to /h/. Ten, hen! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Listen to POT and PAT. Which sound is different in these two words?
questions.hint: The first sound /p/ and the last sound /t/ are the same in both. Stretch out the middle sound of each word.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The middle sound changes from /o/ to /a/. Pot, pat! Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Say "pot" then "pat." The first and last sounds match. Listen to the middle. Try again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The middle sound changes, /o/ to /a/. Pot, pat! Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Read emergent-reader texts with purpose and understanding
standards.parent_tip: Read simple books together and talk about what happened — 'what was the story about?'

questions.prompt:
I see a cat. The cat is big. The cat is on the mat.

Where is the CAT?
questions.hint: Read the last sentence — where is the cat sitting?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The story says the cat is on the mat. Great reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, look back at the story. It tells us right where the cat is. Try again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The story says the cat is on the mat. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
I like dogs. I like cats. I like fish. I like all pets!

What is this STORY about?
questions.hint: Dogs, cats, and fish are all types of...
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The story talks about dogs, cats, and fish. They are all pets! Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Dogs, cats, and fish, what are all of those? Take another look at the story!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The story is all about pets. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
I can run. I can hop. I can skip. I can do a lot!

What CAN'T this person do according to the story?
questions.hint: Read the sentences again. Which action is NOT in the story?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The story says run, hop, and skip, but not fly! Great reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! The story lists run, hop, and skip. Which one is NOT in the story? Try again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The person can't fly, it wasn't in the story. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Mom said, "Get your coat." Dad said, "Get your hat." It is cold outside!

WHY did Mom and Dad tell the child to get a coat and hat?
questions.hint: The last sentence tells you why!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The story says it is cold outside, so they need a coat and hat. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, look back at the story. What did it say about outside? That tells you why. Try again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! They needed a coat and hat because it is cold outside. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Read it: Sam has a red hat. Pam has a red bag. The hat is on the bed.

Who has a red BAG?
questions.hint: Two kids each have a red thing. Read slowly and match the person to the RIGHT object — one has the hat, one has the bag.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The story says Pam has a red bag. Great reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Read it again. One has a hat, one has a bag. Who has the bag? Take another look!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Pam has the red bag. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Read it: The pig is not big. The pig is not sad. The pig is in the mud.

Where is the PIG?
questions.hint: The word 'not' changes the meaning. Find the sentence that tells WHERE the pig is, not what it is or isn't.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The story says the pig is in the mud. Great reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, look back at the story. It tells you just where the pig is. Try again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The pig is in the mud. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Read it: I can hop. I can run. I can not fly. Bugs can fly.

What can I NOT do?
questions.hint: Watch for the words 'can not'. Find the one thing that comes AFTER 'not', and remember bugs are the ones who fly.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The story says I can not fly, only bugs can. Great reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! The story says I can hop and run. Which one can I NOT do? Try again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! I can not fly. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Read it: The sun is up. It is hot. We sit in the shade. The shade is cool.

Why do we SIT in the shade?
questions.hint: The text says the sun is hot but the shade is a different word. Find the word that tells how the SHADE feels.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The story says the shade is cool, so we sit there. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Read it again. What did the story say about the shade? That's the reason. Try again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! We sit in the shade because it is cool. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Read it: Deb fed the hen. The hen sat on an egg. Then the egg let out a chick.

What came out of the EGG?
questions.hint: The hen sat on the egg, but something ELSE came out of it. Read the last sentence and decode the word after 'a'.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The story says a chick came out of the egg. Great reading!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, read the last part again. What came out of the egg? Take another look!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! A chick came out of the egg. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Demonstrate command of the conventions of standard English grammar and usage when writing or speaking (nouns, verbs, plurals, question words, prepositions, complete sentences)
standards.parent_tip: Practice complete sentences at dinner — 'tell me about your day using a full sentence!'

questions.prompt: A NOUN is a person, place, or thing. Which word is a noun?
questions.hint: Can you touch it or point to it? That's a noun!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! "Dog" is a noun, it is a thing, an animal! Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! A noun is a person, place, or thing. Which word names a thing? Take another look!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! "Dog" is the noun, it names an animal. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word in the sentence 'The fish swims in the water' tells what the fish does?
questions.hint: Look for the word that shows the action the fish is doing.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! "Swims" tells what the fish does. It is the action word! Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, read the sentence again. Which word shows the fish moving or doing something? Try again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! "Swims" tells what the fish does. Now you know!

questions.prompt: What is the PLURAL (more than one) of "cat"?
questions.hint: To make most words plural, just add an 's' at the end!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! We add an s to make "cats" for more than one. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! To mean more than one, we usually add a little s. Which one is that? Take another look!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! More than one cat is "cats." We add an s. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word do you use to ask about a PLACE?
questions.hint: "_____ is the park?" — you're asking about a place!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! "Where" asks about a place, like here or there. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about which word we use to ask about a place, like a spot or a room. Try again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! "Where" asks about a place. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Finish the sentence with the correct word: 'There is one mouse by the wall, but I see three ___ by the cheese.'
questions.hint: Some words change in a special way when they mean more than one — they don't just add -s. Say each choice out loud and pick the one that sounds right for grown-up English.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! More than one mouse is "mice." It is a special word! Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! One is a mouse, but more than one has a special word. Say it out loud. Take another look!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! More than one mouse is "mice." Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which question word best fills the blank? '___ do you get to school?' The answer is: 'I ride the bus.'
questions.hint: Match the question word to the answer. 'I ride the bus' tells the WAY you travel, not a place, a person, or a time.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! "How" asks the way you do something, like riding the bus. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, the answer tells the way, riding the bus. Which question word asks in what way? Try again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! "How" asks in what way you do something. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word best fills the blank? 'The squirrel climbed ___ the tree to reach the nuts at the very top.'
questions.hint: A preposition tells where or which direction. Use the clue 'at the very top' to figure out which way the squirrel must go.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The squirrel went up to reach the very top of the tree. That makes sense! Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, the nuts are at the very TOP of the tree. Which way does the squirrel need to climb to reach the top?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The word is 'up.' The top is high, so the squirrel had to climb up. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read this sentence: 'We watched a funny play at school.' In which sentence below does the word 'play' mean the SAME thing?
choices: Do you want to play with me?
Let's play a game of tag.
The children play outside at recess.
Our class put on a play for the parents.
questions.correct: Our class put on a play for the parents.
questions.hint: In the top sentence, 'play' is a THING you watch, not something you do. Find the choice where 'play' is also a thing, not an action.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A play the class puts on is a show, just like the funny play they watched. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! In the first sentence, 'play' is a show you watch. Which sentence talks about a show, not playing a game?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is 'Our class put on a play for the parents.' A play is a show. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which sentence is written correctly?
questions.hint: The sentence is about something that already happened. Some verbs don't add -ed for the past — they change into a new word instead.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'She ran to the store' is just right. 'Ran' is the correct word for something that already happened. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Some of these have a funny word for run. Say each one out loud. Which one sounds right to your ears?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The right sentence is 'She ran to the store.' We say 'ran,' not 'runned.' Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Demonstrate command of the conventions of standard English capitalization, punctuation, and spelling when writing
standards.parent_tip: When writing together, remind about capitals at the start and periods at the end.

questions.prompt: This is a TELLING sentence. Pick the right ending.
questions.hint: A telling sentence (statement) ends with a period!

questions.prompt: Look at the sentence: "Do you like apples" Which mark belongs at the very end?
questions.hint: Remember, a question asks for information. What mark do we use for questions?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Do you like apples' is asking something, so it needs a question mark. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, this sentence is ASKING you something. Which mark do we use when we ask a question?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is a question mark. We use it when a sentence asks something. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Look at this sentence: "My friend is named sam." Which word needs a capital letter?
questions.hint: Remember that a person's name always starts with a big letter.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Sam' is a name, and names always start with a big capital letter. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Look for the word that is a person's NAME. Names get a capital letter. Which word is a name?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The word is 'sam.' It is a name, so it needs a capital S. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read this sentence: "My dog Max ran to me." One word is a NAME, so it must start with a capital letter. Which word is the name?
questions.hint: A name tells you what ONE special person or pet is called, not just what kind of animal it is.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Max' is the dog's name, so it starts with a capital letter. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! One word is the dog's NAME. Names get a capital letter. Which word names the dog?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The name is 'Max.' It is the dog's name, so it gets a capital M. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which sentence is written the correct way?
questions.hint: Check the word 'I' AND check that only the first word gets a capital, not the middle words.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'I see a cat.' has a big I and small letters in the right spots. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! The word 'I' is always big. Cat is not a name, so it stays small. Which one follows both rules?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The right one is 'I see a cat.' The I is big and cat stays small. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Ben jumps up and shouts, "We won the big game___" Which mark best shows that Ben is very excited?
questions.hint: Think about how Ben's voice sounds when he is shouting and excited, not calm.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Ben is very excited, so an exclamation mark shows his big, happy feeling. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, Ben is shouting and SO excited! Which mark shows a big, loud, excited feeling?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is an exclamation mark. We use it for big, excited feelings. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Say the word 'stop' slowly: /s/ /t/ /o/ /p/. Which spelling matches those sounds correctly?
questions.hint: Stretch each sound; the sound right after /s/ is a /t/, even though it can sound a little like a /d/.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! /s/ /t/ /o/ /p/ spells 'stop.' You matched every sound! Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Say the sounds slowly: /s/ /t/ /o/ /p/. Which spelling has a letter for every sound?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The word is 'stop.' The letters s-t-o-p match each sound. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read this: "the sun is hot." Something is not correct. What needs to be fixed?
choices: The first word needs a capital T
It needs a question mark at the end
questions.correct: The first word needs a capital T
questions.hint: Every sentence must START in a special way. Look very closely at the first letter.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The first word 'the' needs a big capital T to start the sentence. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Every sentence must START with a capital letter. Look at the very first word. What is missing?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The first word needs a capital T. Sentences start with a big letter. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Determine or clarify the meaning of unknown and multiple-meaning words and phrases based on kindergarten reading and content
standards.parent_tip: When they encounter new words, look for clues: 'un-happy — un means not, so unhappy means not happy!'

questions.prompt:
The boy JUMPED. He went up in the air.

The **-ed** at the end of "jumped" tells us...
questions.hint: The '-ed' ending tells you about WHEN something happened.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'Jumped' with -ed means it already happened before. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, the -ed ending tells us about the past. Did the jump happen before, or is it coming later?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The -ed means it already happened. The jump is done and over. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
The UNHAPPY girl frowned.

What does **UN-** at the beginning of **unhappy** mean?
questions.hint: Think about words like 'unsafe' or 'unkind' — what does 'un-' add?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! UN- means 'not,' so unhappy means not happy. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! The girl frowned, so she was the opposite of happy. What does UN- do to a word?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! UN- means 'not.' So unhappy means not happy. Now you know!

questions.prompt: "She has two dogs." The **-s** on "dogs" means...
questions.hint: Two dogs is more than one dog. The '-s' is doing that work.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The -s on dogs means more than one dog. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! She has TWO dogs. When we add -s, do we have one thing or lots of them?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The -s means more than one dog. Two is more than one! Now you know!

questions.prompt:
He was HELPFUL at school.

The **-ful** in **helpful** means...
questions.hint: If something is 'colorful' it has lots of color. What does '-ful' usually mean?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! -ful means 'full of,' so helpful means full of help. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, say the word slowly: help-FUL. That -ful sounds like 'full.' What could it mean?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! -ful means full of help. The boy gave lots of help. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
The CARELESS boy spilled his milk.

The **-less** in **careless** means...
questions.hint: If a treat is 'sugarless,' it has no sugar. What does '-less' mean?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! -less means 'without,' so careless means without care. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! The boy spilled his milk because he was not being careful. What might -less mean?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! -less means without care. He had no care and spilled it. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Read this sentence:

"The **bat** flew out of the dark cave when the sun went down."

What does **bat** mean here?
choices: A stick you use to hit a ball
questions.hint: The word 'bat' can mean more than one thing. Use the other words in THIS sentence — 'flew,' 'cave,' 'wings' — to pick the meaning that fits.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The bat flew out of the cave, so it is a flying animal with wings. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! The bat FLEW out of a dark cave. Which meaning of bat can fly? Picture it flying.
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Here bat means a flying animal with wings. It flew from the cave. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Read this sentence:

"**Duck!** The ball is flying right at your head!"

What does **duck** mean here?
choices: To bend down low very fast
questions.correct: To bend down low very fast
questions.hint: 'Duck' can be an animal OR an action. Ask yourself: what would you DO if a ball flew at your head?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The ball is coming at your head, so duck means to bend down fast. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, a ball is flying at your head! What would you do with your body to stay safe?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Here duck means to bend down low very fast, so the ball misses you. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
The rain poured all day. The puppy was not just wet — he was **soaked**.

What does **soaked** most likely mean?
questions.hint: The sentence says 'not just wet.' That tells you 'soaked' is even MORE than plain wet — think about how much.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! It poured rain all day, so the puppy was very, very wet. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! It rained ALL day and the puppy was not just wet. Was he a tiny bit wet, or a whole lot?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Soaked means very, very wet. All that rain got the puppy soaked. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
You know that **UN-** means 'the opposite.'

Mom used a key to **unlock** the door.

What does **unlock** mean?
questions.hint: 'Lock' means to close tight. 'Un-' flips it to the opposite. So what happens to a locked door?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! UN- means the opposite of lock, so unlock means to open a lock. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! UN- means the opposite. Lock keeps a door shut. What is the opposite of that?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Unlock means to open a lock. UN- flips lock to the opposite. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word means the MOST cold — colder than all the others?
questions.hint: Line the words up from a little cold to the MOST cold. Which one is the very strongest?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Freezing is the most cold of all. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! All these words mean cold, but one means the MOST cold. Which one feels like ice?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is 'Freezing.' It means the most cold of all. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: With guidance and support from adults, explore word relationships and nuances in word meanings
standards.parent_tip: Play sorting games with objects and talk about opposites — 'big vs small, hot vs cold.'

questions.prompt: Apple, banana, and orange are alike. Which word is also like them?
questions.hint: Think about what kind of things an apple, a banana, and an orange are.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A grape is a fruit, just like apple, banana, and orange. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, apple, banana, and orange are all FRUITS. Which word on the list is also a fruit you can eat?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is 'grape.' A grape is a fruit, like the others. Now you know!

questions.prompt: What is the OPPOSITE of "hot"?
questions.hint: Opposites are as different as can be. If something isn't hot, it's...
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Cold is the opposite of hot. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Opposite means the total other way. Think about ice. What is the other way from hot?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The opposite of hot is 'Cold.' They are total opposites. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Which word means to walk in a PROUD, fancy way?
questions.hint: Think of a peacock walking proudly — that's strutting!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Strut means to walk in a proud, fancy way. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Which word means to walk tall and proud, not slow or low to the ground?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is 'Strut.' It means to walk in a proud, fancy way. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Dog, cat, and fish all belong to which CATEGORY?
questions.hint: They are all living creatures — what group do they belong to?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Dog, cat, and fish are all animals. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, a dog, a cat, and a fish are all living things. What big group do they all belong to?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! They all belong to 'Animals.' A dog, cat, and fish are animals. Now you know!

questions.prompt: These four words all mean cold: cool, chilly, cold, and freezing. Which word means the MOST cold of all?
questions.hint: Line the words up from a little cold to SO cold your teeth chatter. Which one is the very end?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Freezing means the most cold of all these words. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! These all mean cold, but one is the MOST cold. Which one feels like ice and snow?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is 'Freezing.' It means the most cold of all. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Read this sentence: "The boy swung the bat and hit the ball." What does the word "bat" mean here?
choices: A wooden stick you hit a ball with
A furry animal that flies at night
To blink your eyes very fast
questions.correct: A wooden stick you hit a ball with
questions.hint: Use the other words in the sentence. He SWUNG it to hit a BALL. Which meaning fits that action?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Here the boy hit the ball, so bat means a wooden stick you swing. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! The boy swung it and hit a ball. Which meaning of bat can you hold and swing?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Here bat means a wooden stick you hit a ball with. Now you know!

questions.prompt: A puppy is a baby dog. Which word names a baby cat?
questions.hint: Say each one to yourself: whose baby is it? Match the baby to the CAT, not to a bird, cow, or bear.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A baby cat is called a kitten. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! A puppy is a baby dog. Which word is the special name for a baby cat?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! A baby cat is a 'Kitten.' Just like a baby dog is a puppy. Now you know!

questions.prompt: All of these words mean to eat: nibble, taste, chew, and gobble. Which word means to eat A LOT very fast, like you are super hungry?
questions.hint: Some of these mean eating just a tiny bit. Which one sounds like eating fast and big?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Gobble means to eat a lot really fast. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, these all mean eat, but one means to eat fast when you are super hungry. Which one is the biggest, fastest eating?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is 'Gobble.' It means to eat a lot very fast. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Red, blue, and green are all colors. Which word below is also a color?
questions.hint: The other three words describe things too, but they tell about a feeling, a shape, or a sound. Which one names a color?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Purple is a color, just like red, blue, and green. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Red, blue, and green are all COLORS. Which word on the list is also a color?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is 'Purple.' Purple is a color, like the others. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Use words and phrases acquired through conversations, reading and being read to, and responding to texts
standards.parent_tip: Encourage using new words from books in conversation — 'remember the word enormous? Use it today!'

questions.prompt:
You read a book about the ocean. Now someone asks: "What lives in the ocean?"

The BEST answer is...
questions.hint: Think about what you learned from the ocean book!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Fish, whales, and dolphins all live in the ocean. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! The ocean is full of water. Which animals swim and live in the water?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is fish, whales, and dolphins. They all live in the ocean. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Your teacher says: "Line up in a row."

What does "in a row" mean?
choices: One behind the other in a line
questions.correct: One behind the other in a line
questions.hint: When things are 'in a row,' they make a straight line!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! 'In a row' means standing one behind the other in a line. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about how a line looks when your class lines up. How do you stand in a row?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! 'In a row' means one behind the other in a line. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
You read a book about bears that said bears HIBERNATE in winter.

What does HIBERNATE mean?
questions.hint: Bears go to sleep for the whole winter — that's hibernating!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Hibernate means to sleep for a long time, like bears do in winter. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Bears do this all winter long when it is cold. What do you think hibernate means?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Hibernate means to sleep for a long time. Bears do it all winter. Now you know!

questions.prompt:
Your friend says: "That puzzle is really TRICKY."

What does TRICKY mean?
questions.hint: If something is tricky, you really have to think to solve it!
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Tricky means something is hard to figure out. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! A tricky puzzle is not easy. It makes you think hard. What does tricky mean?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The word tricky means hard to figure out. Now you know!

questions.prompt: When the leaves turn red and yellow and gently fall to the ground, what is another word we can use for this time of year?
questions.hint: Think about the different names we use for the seasons when the weather changes and leaves fall.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! When leaves turn colors and fall, we call that time autumn. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, this is the time when leaves change color and fall down. Which season is another word for fall?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is 'autumn.' It is another word for fall. Now you know!

questions.prompt: After playing in the rain all day, Sam was not just a little wet — he was as wet as a person could ever be. Which word tells that Sam was the MOST wet?
questions.hint: Some words mean a small amount and some mean a big amount. Which one means MORE than just wet?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Soaked means the most wet a person can be. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Sam was not a little wet. He was as wet as could be. Which word means the MOST wet?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is 'Soaked.' It means the most wet of all. Now you know!

questions.prompt: A dog can bark, and a tree can have bark. Read this sentence: "The rough bark on the tree kept its trunk safe." What does the word "bark" mean HERE?
choices: The outside cover of a tree
To yell in a loud voice
questions.correct: The outside cover of a tree
questions.hint: Look at the other words in the sentence, like "tree" and "trunk." Which meaning fits with those?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Here bark means the rough outside cover of a tree. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! This bark is ON a tree and keeps the trunk safe. Which meaning fits a tree?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Here bark means the outside cover of a tree. Now you know!

questions.prompt: The soup just came off the hot stove and was bubbling and steaming. Which word tells that something is the MOST hot?
questions.hint: These words go from cold to very, very hot. Which one is the strongest?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Boiling means the most hot, with bubbles and steam. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, the soup was bubbling and steaming right off the stove. Which word means the MOST hot?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is 'Boiling.' It means the most hot, with bubbles. Now you know!

questions.prompt: The word "trunk" can mean more than one thing. Read this sentence: "The elephant lifted the peanut with its long trunk." What does "trunk" mean in THIS sentence?
choices: The main part of a tree
questions.hint: Think about who is doing the action. What part of an elephant could lift a peanut?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Here trunk means an elephant's long nose. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! The elephant used its trunk to lift a peanut. Which meaning is a part of an elephant?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Here trunk means an elephant's long nose. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Maya got the exact puppy she had always wished for. She was not just a little happy — she was as happy as she could ever be. Which word tells that Maya felt the MOST happy?
questions.hint: Some feeling words are small and some are big. Which one shows the biggest, strongest happy feeling?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Thrilled means the most happy you can feel. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Maya got her wish and was as happy as could be. Which word means the MOST happy?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is 'Thrilled.' It means the most happy of all. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Actively engage in group reading activities with purpose and understanding
standards.parent_tip: Read every day! Mix favorite books with new ones to keep reading exciting.

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: It was group story time. Miss Kim read a book to the class. The best listeners kept their eyes on the pictures and stayed quiet. What is one way to show you are actively listening during group story time?
choices: Talking to your friend next to you.
Looking at the pictures in the book as the teacher reads.
Thinking about what you will do after school.
questions.correct: Looking at the pictures in the book as the teacher reads.
questions.hint: Think about what your body does when you are really paying attention to a story being read aloud with your class.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Looking at the pictures as the teacher reads shows you are listening. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Good listeners stay quiet and keep their eyes on the book. Which choice does that?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is looking at the pictures as the teacher reads. That shows good listening. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: Sam read a story, but some parts got too tricky. He did not give up or quit. He raised his hand and asked his teacher for help. What should you do when a story gets tricky?
questions.hint: When something is hard, what's a smart thing to do?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! When a story gets tricky, you can ask for help, just like Sam did. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, Sam did not give up. He did something smart to keep going. What is a kind thing you can do?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The best thing to do is ask for help. Sam raised his hand. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: The class read a story together. Ben listened closely and understood it well. He raised his hand and asked a good question about what happened. What is one way you can show you understand a story during group reading time?
choices: Talk about a different book you read yesterday.
Ask a question about what happened in the story.
Look at the wall instead of the book.
Keep quiet and not say anything at all.
questions.correct: Ask a question about what happened in the story.
questions.hint: Think about what you do when you hear something in a story that makes you curious or want to share your thoughts.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Asking a question about the story shows you understand it. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Ben understood the story and raised his hand. What can you ask about that will show you get it?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is to ask a question about what happened in the story. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: Miss Ray read a story to the whole class. As she read, she asked questions about it. The engaged children raised their hands and answered them. How can you show you are actively engaged when your teacher is reading a story to the whole class?
choices: Talk to your friend about your lunch
Look at the clock on the wall
questions.hint: Think about what it means to be part of the story and show you understand when everyone is reading together.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Answering the teacher's questions shows you are engaged in the story. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! Miss Ray asked questions while she read. What can you do to show you are paying attention?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The answer is to answer questions about the story. That shows you are engaged. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Our class read a story about a dog named Pip. In the story, Pip lost his red ball. Pip looked and looked, but he could not find it. His tail hung down low. How does Pip feel in the story?
questions.hint: The words do not say how Pip feels. Look for clues, like what happened to his ball and what his tail is doing.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Pip lost his ball and his tail hung low, so he feels sad. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, Pip could not find his ball and his tail hung down low. How do you feel when you lose something?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Pip feels sad. He lost his ball and his tail hung low. Now you know!

questions.prompt: The teacher read us a story. In it, the sky filled with dark gray clouds. Right away, the children in the story ran inside to grab their coats. Why did the children run to grab their coats?
choices: They wanted to play a game
questions.hint: Think about what dark gray clouds usually mean, then think about why you would want a coat.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Dark gray clouds mean rain is coming, so the children got their coats. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Take another look! The sky filled with dark gray clouds. What happens outside when you see clouds like that?
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The children ran because it was going to rain. Dark clouds bring rain. Now you know!

questions.prompt: During story time we read about Mia. Mia planted one tiny seed in the dirt. She gave it water every single day. Now a red flower grows where the seed was. What made the flower grow?
choices: Mia gave it water every day
questions.correct: Mia gave it water every day
questions.hint: Read all the sentences. Find what Mia did over and over before the flower came.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Mia gave the seed water every single day, so it grew into a flower. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about what Mia did every single day to help her tiny seed. Give it another try!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The flower grew because Mia gave it water every day. Water helps plants grow. Now you know!

questions.prompt: In our book, Sam gave a big yawn. He put on his soft pajamas. Outside his window the sky was dark, and many stars were out. What time is it in the story?
questions.hint: You cannot see a clock. Use three clues together: the pajamas, the dark sky, and the stars.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The sky was dark and the stars were out, so it is nighttime. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Think about the dark sky and all those stars. When do we see those? Try again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! It is nighttime in the story. Dark skies and stars come out at night. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Our class read about a boy named Ben. A new kid sat down with no snack and looked sad. Ben walked over and gave the new kid half of his own snack. Then they both smiled. Why did Ben share his snack?
choices: The new kid had no snack
Ben did not like his snack
questions.correct: The new kid had no snack
questions.hint: Look at what you learned about the new kid right before Ben shared.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Ben saw the new kid had nothing to eat, so he shared to be kind. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Think about why the new kid looked sad. What did that kid not have? Look again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Ben shared because the new kid had no snack. He wanted to be kind. Now you know!

standards.standard_description: Actively engage in group reading activities with informational texts with purpose and understanding
standards.parent_tip: Pick fact books about what your child loves — animals, weather, trucks, food.

questions.prompt: If we read a fact book that showed us pictures of lions, tigers, and bears, what would be the main topic of that book?
questions.hint: Think about what lions, tigers, and bears all are. The main topic tells you what the whole book is mostly about.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Lions, tigers, and bears are all wild animals. That is the main topic. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, think about what lions, tigers, and bears all are. What do they have in common? Try again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The main topic is wild animals. Lions, tigers, and bears are all wild animals. Now you know!

questions.prompt: You are reading a fact book about bugs with your group. What is a good way to actively engage with the book?
choices: Tell your friend a secret joke.
Ask your teacher a question about a bug in the book.
Close your eyes and listen to music.
Draw a picture of a flower on your hand.
questions.correct: Ask your teacher a question about a bug in the book.
questions.hint: Think about how you show you are interested and learning when you are reading with your friends and teacher.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Asking a question about a bug helps you learn more from the book. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! Which choice helps you learn about the bugs in the book? Take another look!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The best way is to ask your teacher a question about a bug. Asking helps you learn! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Your teacher is reading a book to help everyone learn about different animals. What is the best way to show you are learning?
choices: Close your eyes and try to guess what happens next.
Talk to your friend about playing outside later.
Look at the pictures and listen carefully to the words about animals.
Draw a picture of your favorite toy while the teacher reads.
questions.correct: Look at the pictures and listen carefully to the words about animals.
questions.hint: Think about what you do with your eyes and ears when you want to learn from a book.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Looking at the pictures and listening carefully helps you learn about animals. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Which choice keeps your eyes and ears on the animal book? Look again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The best way is to look at the pictures and listen carefully to the words. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: Our group read a fact book about the sea. It had interesting pictures of fish and whales. We talked about the pictures together and learned new things. When your group reads a fact book, what is a good way to learn new things together?
choices: Only look at the words, not the pictures.
Keep all your good ideas quiet inside your head.
Talk about the interesting pictures with your friends.
Quickly turn all the pages without stopping.
questions.correct: Talk about the interesting pictures with your friends.
questions.hint: Think about how talking with others can help everyone understand new facts from a book.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Talking about the pictures with your friends helps you all learn new things. Great sharing!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! Which choice helps you and your friends learn together? Take another look!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The best way is to talk about the pictures with your friends. Sharing helps you learn! Now you know!

questions.prompt: Listen to this fact page: "Bees live in a hive. The hive keeps bees safe. A hive has many little rooms. Bees build the hive from wax." What is this page MOSTLY about?
questions.hint: Which thing is talked about in almost every sentence?
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Every sentence tells us about the hive. That is what the page is mostly about. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, listen again. Almost every sentence talks about the same thing. What is it? Try again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The page is mostly about a hive. Each sentence tells us about it. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Listen to this fact page: "A turtle has a hard shell. When a big dog comes close, the turtle pulls its head inside. It stays there until the dog walks away." Why does the turtle pull its head inside?
choices: To stay safe from the dog
questions.correct: To stay safe from the dog
questions.hint: Think about what happens right before the turtle hides, and what happens after.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The turtle hides its head to stay safe from the dog. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Good try! Think about the big dog coming close. Why would the turtle want to hide? Look again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! The turtle pulls its head in to stay safe from the dog. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Listen to this fact page: "Owls sleep all day long. Owls wake up when it is night. Owls look for food when they are awake." When do owls look for food?
choices: In the middle of the day
questions.hint: First find when owls are awake, then use that to find when they look for food.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! Owls wake up when it is night, so that is when they look for food. Great listening!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Almost! The page says owls wake up at one special time. When is that? Try again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Owls look for food at night. They wake up when it is dark. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Listen to this fact page: "A camel can go many days with no water. A fish must stay in water all the time. Both a camel and a fish need food to live." How are a camel and a fish the SAME?
choices: They both must stay in water
They both live in the sea
They both go days with no water
questions.hint: Most sentences tell how they are different. Look for the one thing they share.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! A camel and a fish both need food to live. That is how they are the same. Great job!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Hmm, listen for the part that says BOTH. What do a camel and a fish both need? Take another look!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! A camel and a fish are the same because they both need food. Now you know!

questions.prompt: Listen to this fact book: "This book shows how a seed grows. First a seed is planted in the dirt. Next it gets water and sun. Then a tiny green plant pokes up." What would this book MOST likely tell you NEXT?
choices: How the little plant grows bigger and makes flowers
How to bake a loaf of bread
What the plant looked like as a seed
questions.correct: How the little plant grows bigger and makes flowers
questions.hint: The book goes in order. Think about what comes after a tiny plant pokes up.
questions.correct_feedback: Yes! The plant just poked up, so next it would grow bigger and make flowers. Great thinking!
questions.incorrect_feedback: Close one! The tiny plant just poked up. What would happen to it next? Try again!
questions.reveal_feedback: That one was tricky! Next the book would tell how the plant grows bigger and makes flowers. Now you know!
